it's great we're getting started on this. we have a great opportunity here to design an API that lasts and is nice to use :-)  i have a few comments and questions, happy to discuss.
 
well done on the performance measurement and caching! do we have an idea of how many clients are cached after, for example, the integration test suite has run through?

## external vs internal clients

i'm still a bit confused external and internal clients. i understand that we need:

* a client that talks to ASF services
* a client that talks to some other AWS compatible backend (like proxying to dynamodb). so basically a regular botocore client.

but why does the say "Build and return client for connections **originating** outside LocalStack". Aren't we connect from localstack to an "external" service?

sorry for the stupid question. can you briefly outline the difference and what they are used for? i feel we could use this as an opportunity to create more clarity for other developers as well.

## connect to

from localstack.aws.connect import connect_to
```python
connect_to('sns', source_service='s3', target_arn='arn:aws:sns:ap-south-1:000000000000:lorem')\
.publish(TopicArn='arn:aws:sns:ap-south-1:000000000000:lorem', message='blah')
```

just thinking out loud: does the `target_arn` always appear somewhere in the request? could we generalize and hide this from the user by defining a set of declarations? e.g., `target_arn_selector[("sns", "TopicArn")] = "TopicArn"`? or is this too inflexible/complicated/hard to maintain?

in that case, there would also be a way to generalize the `connect_to` signature, which would give us a way to generalize the client factory interface.
so instead of having a `ConnectFactory` with `get_internal_client` and `get_external_client`, we'd have a `ASFClientFactory` and an `BotoClientFactory`

LGTM overall, but just to clarify:

1. When working with a future representing the return object of a task, future cancellation will not cancel the corresponding task, and we'd need a tighter concurrent.futures integration (e.g. Ray implementing a concurrent.futures executor) in order to support that, right?
2. In the same vein, `future.running()` will always be `False`, even if the task that produces the future is currently running.

Are there any concerns with not supporting the semantics of part of the `concurrent.futures.Future` API? My first impression is that this is fine for now, and that this shouldn't be blocked by a proper `concurrent.Futures` API.

Thanks @maukoquiroga for this exciting proposal!

- It relies on the fact that the two templates do not have any dependency that would not be provided by the Core. At this stage (and for the foreseeable future), this is the case: they both only depend on Core. However, this should be documented at least in a comment.
- The current README install procedure for development mode suggests `pip install --editable .[dev] --use-deprecated=legacy-resolver`. If I understand correctly, one would now need to use `make install` (which is consistent with https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-core/issues/1040), so this should be updated. A workaround would be to make sure that `make test` installs these test dependencies.
- We lose the versioning of templates in testing, which means that builds are not reproducible: they might pass or fail one day and yield a different result with a different Template version. This is a regression, but I find it an acceptable cost considering that this is an edge case because of the slow change pace of Template.
- Can you please confirm that this solves https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-core/issues/1158, and link it appropriately if that's the case? 🙂

I really think Pants should define a model if not a format. Presumably, internally, it will map all resolve graph information into that model before rendering, allowing JVM, Python, etc backends to map to a model and the core graphing goal to do the rendering in 1 spot in a consistent way that even in the future allows for mixed-language graphs. Definition of that model would help ensure the Pex format defined here need not thrash, since Pants needs have been thought through and worked out as evidenced by the model.

From reading https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/issues/10798 it sounds like we could potentially better address this by incrementally dirtying, rather than skipping dirtying entirely... I'll outline why I'm concerned about the approach in this PR, and then describe how I think incremental dirtying could look in that example...

Reduced example (and we had some of this discussion on https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/pull/9015 :)):

`IsSameAsGoldenFile` reads a source file, and compares it with the copy of that source file on a branch whose name was specified in another file:

```
                                    SomeGoal
                                       |
                                  SomeOtherRule
                                       |
                                IsSameAsGoldenFile
                                  /            \   
ReadFileFromSourceTree("path/in/repo")   ReadFileFromBranch("path/in/repo", output_of(dep))
                                                             |   
                             GetGitShaFromBranchName("path/of/name/of/golden/branch", uncacheable=true)
```

Let's say `IsSameAsGoldenFile` has already executed in this run, because it has pleasantly few dependencies.

Then let's say `path/in/repo` changes in the source tree (say, because it was changed by formatter).

With this PR as it stands, I believe the semantics are to say "We already ran `IsSameAsGoldenFile` in this build, because it happens to have a transitive dep on `GetGitShaFromBranchName` we won't re-run it, even though that's unrelated to the reason we _should_ dirty it". So we're going to return a stale result for `IsSameAsGoldenFile`.

In general, I think this is a symptom of the semantics of relying on re-running nodes in response to filesystem change during a build. But if we're signed up to those semantics, I think we need to honour them in this situation. While "You wrote an uncacheable node, so you get different semantics for that node" feels reasonable for a rule author, "You happen to transitively depend on an uncacheable node in another ruleset which you don't know about, so you get different semantics" feels like it will be very surprising to both rule-authors and users.

I _think_ what this PR is trying to avoid is having to (potentially repeatedly) traverse up the tree to mark `SomeOtherRule` and `SomeGoal` as dirty, and then, if `IsSameAsGoldenFile` happened to be the same result, to re-mark them as clean - it's not intentionally trying to avoid re-evaluating nodes which actually need to be re-evaluated, that's just an accidental side-effect of trying to do all the dirty/clean book-keeping (have we observed this to be a problem, by the way? Or is this just defensive?).

Something I think we could do differently to achieve the same aim (and I haven't thought this through much), is to not eagerly transitively dirty things. So when `ReadFileFromSourceTree` is invalidated, it _only_ dirties `IsSameAsGoldenFile`. Then when we re-evaluate `IsSameAsGoldenFile`, if the result is the same, we're done, but if the result is different, we'd then dirty `SomeOtherRule`, and so on. This may cause us to perform work we're going to throw away (e.g. if we evaluate `SomeOtherRule` thinking `IsSameAsGoldenFile` is clean, but then find out it's dirty so throw away its result), so it may suggest we'd want to de-prioritise the potentially-dirty call-stacks if there are other call-stacks we could be evaluating, but eventually the dirtying will propagate up far enough that we should never _finish_ with stale results.

I noticed a few small things and then one bigger breaking thing caused by running `black` on some files.

_Small things and some nits:_

Since we're updating the `Makefile` I compared it with the python project template `Makefile` and noticed we could add / change a few things:

- `find . -name '__pycache__' -exec rm -rf {} +` at the end of the `clean-pyc` command
- We could split out the `validate-docs` command from there if we wanted to have that separated
- `make notes` is missing "build" from the towncrier line, it should be: `towncrier build --yes --version $(UPCOMING_VERSION)`
- nit cleanup (since both seem to work): `tox elint` -> `tox e lint` in `make lint` command

I also noticed some warnings when running `make notes` related to titles in the docstrings [here](https://github.com/ethereum/eth-abi/blob/master/eth_abi/exceptions.py#L27) and [here](https://github.com/ethereum/eth-abi/blob/master/eth_abi/exceptions.py#L45) that can be silenced by just removing the `-----------` which makes it a title. `black` also seems to be happy with that so this works. Unless we want to keep that formatting in which case adding one more `-` since it is too short reduces the noise from the warning to just an "unexpected title" and it isn't as loud of a warning (not bright red). I'd be good with removing them altogether or extending.

---
_Big thing caused by `black` commit:_

I noticed that `bumpversion` can't find the bump version anymore because it annoyingly only looks for single quotes in the line `version='3.0.0'` and we (maybe accidentally?) ran `black` on `setup.py` which changed it to double quotes. We should change it back to single quotes as `version='3.0.0'` and then it does find the version correctly.

We also ran `black` on `docs/conf.py` which changed [this line](https://github.com/ethereum/eth-abi/blob/master/docs/conf.py#L26-L26) which we will need to change back to `setup_version = line.split("\'")[1]` once the above `version` change is made.

Unless there's another way to get around it, but currently those are the changes I made locally that got it all building.

looks great overall! just a couple of questions/nits

one broader questions i want to clarify is what assumptions we are making when accessing the payload of the request object.

if the request is a form request and contains a form-encoded body, the following calls will return
```python
request.get_data(as_text=True) # reads from the stream, caches the body, and returns it
request.form                   # parses the form data from the cache and returns the form data
request.get_data(as_text=True) # returns the cached body (will contain the original form-encoded body!)
````

whereas calling:
```python
request.data                   # reads from the stream, parses the form data, caches the form data
                               # and caches the remaining body (which is now empty, and will return `""`)
request.get_data(as_text=True) # reads and returns the cached body (which is empty)
request.form                   # returns the cached form data
request.get_data(as_text=True) # reads and returns the cached body (which is empty)
```

see here for a detailed explanation: https://github.com/pallets/werkzeug/blob/bb21bf90b0b121e3ed45b9950b823e4b43a81fd8/src/werkzeug/wrappers/request.py#L385-L390

so, the parser will potentially behave differently depending on previous calls to `request.data` or `request.get_data(parse_form_data=True)`.
we definitely don't want that, but i'm not sure how to address it.

after writing all this and thinking a bit more about the werkzeug API, i think it may be the best call to NOT call `get_data` at all, and just rely on the properties `request.data`, `request.values`, `request.form`, `request.args`, etc, which seems to be the way it was intended.
you lose the bit of convenience that `as_text=True` provides, but at then the behavior will be consistent.

Hello @thedodd.

I agree, that the `static_primary` feature is useful and there is a certain demand for it from the community - I've been asked many times about the possibility to run clusters with Patroni, but without automatic failover, because people often want to simplify configuration management.

Right now the feature is very limited, and supports only single-node clusters, but I don't see any problem that it could be extended to support clusters of arbitrary size:
- Node that thinks that is it configured as static_primary should continue running as primary (or promote) even if DCS is down/not accessible or updated_lock()/acquire_lock() calls have failed.
- Other nodes should always use the static_primary instead of whatever is stored in the leader key in DCS.
- We can also support a switchover via Patroni REST API/patronictl. It will simply change the static_primary field in the config.
- If the static_primary value is changed in DCS, the new node should not promote until the old one is shut down cleanly. We can promote though if the old one isn't accessible.
- We need to document that in case of a network spilt it is the responsibility of the user to do STONITH of the isolated old primary. Such a situation could happen for example with Etcd, where we don't use quorum reads - the old static_primary will continue to see the stale data.

I really like the idea, but I think some changes are needed (in addition to the minor ones in the comments below this one):
1. If a history-tracked model happens to have a `change_reason` field, this code will cause problems.. I guess this can be solved by either:
    1. dynamically detecting this and making sure that this code's `change_reason` field has a non-colliding name
    2. renaming this code's `change_reason` to e.g. `history_change_reason` (since it's already [listed as a reserved field name](https://django-simple-history.readthedocs.io/en/latest/common_issues.html#reserved-field-names))
2. A subclass of `SimpleHistoryAdmin` that overrides the `form` field will have a `FieldError` raised after `get_fieldsets()` adds the `change_reason` field, which means that multiple existing codebases will likely break. This should be solved by either detecting it in the code, or by documenting it - see the point below.
3. All of this should be documented so that users know that the `change_reason` form field exists and is added by default. Instructions on how to disable/override the behavior should also be documented - and potentially implemented.
4. It would be nice to have a test checking that the `change_reason` field is correctly set when *reverting* an object to a previous history record (since the `change_reason` field appears in the revert form as well).
   Some tests for the problem scenarios mentioned in the points above would also be nice 🙂

Lastly, thank you for taking the initiative to implement this! 😊

Your thoughtfulness is excellent.
I am using my free time to this PR because your proposal deals with how methods should be invoked with what args, that is also adjacent to static typing.

I am still concerned about the large number of `if` clauses in the `TestEntry`([This SO explains why avoiding such things](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/27880631/why-should-you-avoid-conditional-logic-in-unit-tests-and-how)).
In particular, `mock_function` reproduces the behavior of `WINFUNCTYPE`(is my recognition right?).
But we need to ensure the correctness of this.

Rather, I prefer to create and inspect a mock that assumes the objects returned by the "raw" `WINFUNCTYPE`.
Following test code is hastily prepared, so it may be flawed in some ways, but please point them out to me if so.

```py
from comtypes import COMMETHOD
from ctypes import HRESULT

WSTRING = c_wchar_p
...

class Test_FixInOutArgs(ut.TestCase):
    def test_PortableDeviceApiLib_CreateObjectWithPropertiesAndData(self):
        spec = COMMETHOD(
            [],
            HRESULT,
            "CreateObjectWithPropertiesAndData",
            (["in"], POINTER(IUnknown), "pValues"),  # IPortableDeviceValues
            (["out"], POINTER(POINTER(IUnknown)), "ppData"),  # IStream
            (["in", "out"], POINTER(c_ulong), "pdwOptimalWriteBufferSize"),
            (["in", "out"], POINTER(WSTRING), "ppszCookie"),
        )
        m = MagicMock()
        fixed = _fix_inout_args(m, spec.argtypes, spec.paramflags)
        p_val = MagicMock(
            spec=POINTER(IUnknown), name="POINTER(IPortableDeviceValues)"
        )()
        pp_data = MagicMock(name="POINTER(POINTER(IStream))")()
        buf_size = MagicMock(spec=POINTER(c_ulong), name="POINTER(c_ulong)")()
        cookie = MagicMock(spec=POINTER(WSTRING), name="POINTER(c_wchar_p)")()
        m.return_value = (pp_data, ..., ...)
        ret_val = fixed(p_val, buf_size, cookie)
        self.assertEqual(ret_val, [pp_data, 1, None])
        m.assert_called_once()
        (m_1st, m_2nd, m_3rd), m_kw = m.call_args
        self.assertEqual(m_1st, p_val)
        self.assertEqual(m_2nd, buf_size)
        self.assertIsInstance(m_3rd, c_ulong)
        self.assertEqual(list(m_kw), ["ppszCookie"])  # ensure length and keys
        self.assertIsInstance(m_kw["ppszCookie"], c_wchar_p)
```
This PR deals with a difficult problem for test-driven development.
My code is only a suggestion, and there should be many different test perspectives.

Any opinions also would be appreciated.

However, I'm not really convinced by this feature in the first place:
* It's a rather rare use case IMO, meaning we set a "default" which is rarely correct. In most cases, the target is named `synth` or `sim`, and the toplevel will have a very different name.
* This default will make it harder for users to see/debug cases where they failed to specify a toplevel, and now will get errors from their synthesis/simulation tool about e.g. the toplevel "sim" not found, something they never really specified. (Yes, there's an INFO Message, but EDA tool users have been trained over the decades to mostly ignore those.)

You can add a class in `StatusWidget` (the parent element) within the [constructor](https://github.com/jupyterlab/jupyterlab-git/blob/master/src/components/StatusWidget.tsx#L27) by adding the following line:

```ts
this.addClass('jp-git-StatusWidget')
```

Note: by design it is not possible to select parent of an element with CSS selectors.

> Another issue is when navigate to another repo (change file directory), the branch name stays unless you click on the git extension icon on the left menu bar, which is a bit weird.

The default behavior is to not refresh the Git model status when the sidebar is hidden to avoid useless calls to the backend. You could solve this by forcing refreshing the branch calling [GitExtension.refreshBranch](https://github.com/jupyterlab/jupyterlab-git/blob/master/src/model.ts#L981) when we set a [new repository](https://github.com/jupyterlab/jupyterlab-git/blob/master/src/index.ts#L151).

> 
> @adehad @ssbarnea some architectural guidance is welcome :-)

@rynkk, thanks for this. Especially as this is one of the more convoluted areas in the code.
As suggested by @studioj I believe this batch size needs to be set at a class instance level. i.e. in here something like DEFAULT_BATCH_SIZE
https://github.com/pycontribs/jira/blob/678903805e34d91c3905d73d20fcc7a3f5c8036c/jira/client.py#L343

I completely understand where you are coming from with wanting to set this per function. It provides the most flexibility, especially as you will likely have more issues than dashboards and therefore would want to set it differently for different resources. Also scales differently depending on how fast the server is you are working with.

A solution I would prefer if you strongly would like to set this per endpoint might look like so:

```python
# within the DEFAULT_OPTIONS

# A dict of (Resource Type, Batch Size) 
DEFAULT_BATCH_SIZE: Dict[Resource, int] = {
    Issue : 500,
    Resource: 100, # or 50 or whatever we want as a default now
}


...

# inside the JIRA.__init__

self.options["default_batch_size"] = DEFAULT_BATCH_SIZE
self.options["default_batch_size"].update(default_batch_sizes)  # or whatever the argument name is
 



...
# now inside fetch pages

        if maxResults:
            page_params["maxResults"] = maxResults
        else: # will need to set batch size to get all resources
            page_params["maxResults"] = self.options["default_batch_size"].get(item_type, self.options["default_batch_size"].get(Resource))
            _LOG.verbose(f"Batch size for {item_type.__name__} set to {page_params["maxResults"]}")
```

There might be problems with this approach too, but I feel it may be more ergonomic for advanced users such as yourself without confusing/bogging down more casual users

Hi there everybody, and thanks for the contributions. We'll be releasing this under a new major version as there are a significant amount of breaking changes in here (mostly looking at the Python >2 change).
Sorry for the late review! I had a couple questions

A few comments from my side: 

So you are caching multiple processor instances, giving them time to parse parameters and load models into memory. I suppose this is all meant for the single-worker model carved out by Triet? I am asking because in multi-threading/processing it will be problematic to "steal" vital runtime objects like `Process.workspace` and `Process.input_file_grp` from the existing/cached instances. But in any case IMO you still need some function for cache cleaning. (And for GPU-enabled processors, disallowing caching or setting the cache size at runtime in order to prevent GPURAM OOM might be necessary, too.)

Furthermore, comparing to my own [refactoring and networking](https://github.com/OCR-D/core/pull/652) from 2yrs ago, a couple of differences stick out. (Omitting the networking side:)
- your PR offers multiple instances each by their own parameters instead of just one
- my PR also exposes `run_api` for what in your case would be `run_processor(...cached_processor=True)`; I recommend offering the same interface (i.e. `get_processor` plus `run_api`
- my PR catches runtime exceptions and allows to address them differently via `run_api`
- my PR encapsulates the CWD changes necessary during processing (storing the old CWD prior to the run, and restoring it afterwards, even in the case of failure); I believe your current draft will fail resolving input files when caching is allowed
- my PR also addresses the `task_sequence.ProcessorTask` API: it tries to find the class and instance from the executable name (this part is naturally messy), then uses the actual Processor instance for parameter validation (and even avoiding `--dump-json` callouts); not sure you want this, but then clearly the Processor class itself should expose parameter valdiation (currently only in the constructor); also, IMO it is worthwhile on your side already thinking about how to backport the `task_sequence.run_tasks` functionality (i.e. `ocrd process`) for processing servers (as a minimalistic/fallback/prototype use-case)

Nice! I noticed a few tests in the `tests/core/utilities` and `tests/core/filtering` directories where you changed `w3_strict_abi` -> `w3`, but not the other way around, so there are some duplicate tests now, I believe. I commented on a few of them, but not all. 

> 1. The strict bytes type check flag is now able to be toggled on and off. I think this is a plus but open to discussion there as I took that liberty of my own accord. I've added checks to make sure the codec makes sense when it is passed on to other properties. In modules, for example, the codec is inherited from the w3 instance. I think this makes sense but a setter could also easily be added for more module-specific customization.

This makes sense to me!

> 2.  w3.ens inherits the codec and strict_bytes_type_checking flag as well since this module is on the web3 instance itself. Previously, this was not a thing and I'm not sure this was ideal. Let's say a user turned on strict bytes checking for the w3 instance and used the w3.ens. I would expect to have strict bytes checking on for the ens module as well, but, under the hood this would call an internal init_web3 which uses the w3 instances provider and middlewares but that's it. So strict byte checking would not be on. These changes make it so that the w3.ens.w3 property is an exact object reference back to the w3 instance. This means that whatever settings change on the w3 instance itself are directly reflected onto w3.ens.w3 as well since this is tied to the same object reference now.
> 3.   ENS.from_web3 only inherits the codec and strict byte check flag value at the time that the ENS class is instantiate. I think this makes perfect sense since this is a way to instantiate a standalone ENS class object. It does not seem ideal at all to inherit the object reference here since changing ns.w3.strict_bytes_type_checking, for example, would also change w3.strict_bytes_type_checking.

Feel free to take or leave since I think either way we leave it will be sort of confusing, but IMO it's unintuitive to have `ENS.from_web3(w3).w3` be a different web3 instance than `w3.ens.w3`. Although I do agree that it’s also confusing to have `ns.w3.strict_bytes_type_checking` have the side effect of also changing the flag on the w3 instance. I think what’s throwing me off is that the method is called `from_web3`. Maybe it would be more intuitive to have the strict_bytes_type_checking flag on the ENS instance instead? Or, if we do decide to stick with diverging w3 instances, I think it’s worth adding a note to the `from_web3` docs to say that the web3 instance associated with the `ns` instance is a new instance than the one that was passed to `from_web3`.

I think it would be helpful first to define a dataclass `Resource` which has cpus, gpus, memory, disk, queue.  Then each `worker` object can be converted into a `Resource` and the bundle we're trying to schedule can be converted into a `Resource`.  Then we can define a function `def dominates(self, other: Resource)` which returns True if each dimension (cpus, gpus, etc.) for `self` is at least as great as `other`.  Then,
1) We compute the list of feasible workers (those that dominate the bundle).  If this is non-empty, we're done.
2) If not, then we take all the workers and keep only those workers that aren't dominated by another worker.  For each of these potential workers, we generate a string (`worker_resource.render_shortcomings(bundle_resource)`) which lists all the dimensions where worker_resource falls short.  For example, if worker_resource has gpus=2 and bundle_resource has gpus=4, then we'd generate something like "2 GPUs (4 requested)".  So then the final staged status would be something like "Insufficient resources, but the following workers are available: ...".

| 7 | edit other parts of `BUILD`, e.g. comments | :x: | :x: | :white_check_mark: |

\* For the specific case of editing tags, I don't think this could ever change the runtime behaviour(?), so arguably no restart would be better behaviour... but that seems like a detail specific to only a few fields, and doesn't apply to all situations (e.g. `--filter-tag-regex` target filtering means tag changes can change the behaviour for other goals, as tested below).

Run `PANTS_SOURCE=... pants test ::`, and while it is running:

| # |action | should restart? | actually restart? | pass |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 8 | edit `test_fast.py` | only `test_fast.py` | both `test_fast.py` and `test_slow.py` | :x: |
| 9 | edit `test_slow.py` | only `test_slow.py` | both `test_fast.py` and `test_slow.py` | :x: |

Run `PANTS_SOURCE=... pants test --filter-tag-regex=some_tag ::`, and while it is running:

| # | action | expected behaviour | actual behaviour | pass |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 10 | add `some_tag` to `test_fast.py` | `test_fast.py` is run, `test_slow.py` continues running | `test_fast.py` is run (`test_slow.py` also restarts, covered by 8) | :white_check_mark: |
| 11 | remove `some_tag` from `test_slow.py` | `test_slow.py` is interrupted and the test run edits immediately | as expected | :white_check_mark: |

My general comment is: nice!

Broadly speaking though, it feels like there's quite a lot of `unwrap`ing. I've commented on some specific ones, but there's also a lot of `name.named_child(...).unwrap()` or equivalent. I'd be nervous about this making dep inference fragile to invalid code, or syntax changes, or other unexpected problems, and bringing down the whole pantsd when they fail.

Some alternatives, depending on the situation:

- propagate errors with `?` etc.
- `.expect("<short explanation>")` or `unwrap_or_with(|| panic!("foo {some_value} bar"))` to at least give more context
- ignore silently or with some logging (e.g. if the code is invalid)

>    If you have any idea how to directly reuse the added code in the existing find_files(pageId=...) selector (so there will be no duplication), let me know.

The hard part about integrating the added code to find_files is that find_files is cluttered with many different cases. Almost all parameters can be either a string or a pattern. IMO, we should split that method into smaller methods for the separate search cases.

Consider this method invocation: find_files(fileGrp='DEFAULT'). Although we just want to get all files that belong to that file group without other search parameters, we must still iterate over all file elements of the file group to check the other search parameters even when they are not presented. So the invocation above still has a linear execution time instead of a constant time where we could just return the cached list.

Hi @kvignesh1420,

the change looks sensible to me under the condition that there is no standard TensorFlow Optimizer that has an attribute `_iterations`, but not `iterations`.

As far as I can tell that assumption should hold:

- For `OptimizerV2` the property `iterations()` has been added before TF version 1.12.1 (https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/commit/1a4e13725f04d8b27ed8f6c5feeed5ea7b2e54e9) and we only support TF 1.15 and TF 2.
- TF1-style `Optimizer` does not have a member variable `_iterations` at all. I can't find that string anywhere in `tensorflow/python/training/optimizer.py` or neighboring files like `adam.py` in a search on TF master: https://cs.opensource.google/search?q=_iterations&sq=&ss=tensorflow%2Ftensorflow:tensorflow%2Fpython%2Ftraining%2F
